meta.title: Solana Founders: What We Care About Now

transcript:
Mary Gooneratne: All right, hi everyone. It is awesome to be here. That was an exciting intro. I feel like it encapsulates the energy of this week. Um, and this for me personally is my fifth Breakpoint, so it's an honor to be on stage here with you guys. Um, and kind of thinking back to that first Breakpoint, the space has changed dramatically. And kind of my first question for you guys was just as a personal reflection, what you guys are most proud of, um, in terms of accomplishments over the past five years, thinking back to that Breakpoint?

Raj Gokal: Yeah, um, I'll go first. I think, um, you know, just the the constant reinvention of the the network, the the ecosystem, and and the community. Um, I, you know, a lot of people just don't even remember, but Solana started as like a a ghost chain, and then uh a DeFi chain, and then an FTX chain, and then a meme coin, well NFT chain, that was a whole cycle, and then meme coins, and now it's clear that, you know, it's used for absolutely everything and it it, um, there's a compounding effect of all of those things coming together. So I think, uh, you know, the reason the community uh has the resilience to go through all that reinvention is is because they're here for a reason, for each other and and, you know, for the performance and, um, and and the ease of use of the network.

Anatoly Yakovenko: Um, I I'm I'm really proud that I only have like seven minutes of stage time. They were discussing how it that their goal next year is to have no stage time. I want to be in the audience. I mean, I think, uh, one of my favorite things that Balaji has said that, um, decentralization is not the absence of leadership, it's the abundance of leadership. And over the last five years, just so many people have stepped up from Foundation and and Labs, but even folks completely outside of any of those, um, kind of original, um, organizations, just community people, people building applications and protocols, have really took the leadership roles uh for the entire network. To the point that, you know, I don't have GitHub commit privileges anymore and I spent like two minutes on stage. Um, my goal is to just be in the audience. That would be like, I think, seeing the network really mature and take a life of its own.

Mary Gooneratne: And kind of uh going off of the point you were making, Raj, about all these different evolutions Solana has taken, I think especially over the past year we've seen all these different use cases, but there's been these two major kind of themes emerging both around kind of memes and that use case as well as kind of in parallel the institutionalization and the launch of the ETFs. And I'm curious how you guys think about all those different use cases, um, like interplaying with each other as well as how you guys think about embracing them or managing them.

Anatoly Yakovenko: I think the internet has always been, um, both ex very serious and create a lot of like kind of value that's very easy to understand, and also entertainment. And, you know, the famous quote is "internet is for cats." Um, and if you look at like technology in general, Nvidia started as a video game company. So I think memes and NFTs and all these things are kind of the the cats part of the internet. But because of those, we're able to build and develop technology that can really serve global finance, um, as cheap as physics allow. And I think that that's really cool that we we get to do both.

Raj Gokal: Yeah, I I remember the first time I set up an email address was because my cousin in South Africa wanted to send me the dancing baby GIF. And like, that's why I got an email address. And, you know, it's why a lot of people got an email address. And I think meme coins might be the the first reason a lot of people get a wallet, but, um, it grows into something much larger.

Mary Gooneratne: Makes a lot of sense. And I agree, I feel like you see that even with how people use the chain today and what they get their first Phantom wallet for. Um, and so thinking about kind of how you guys are looking towards next year, uh I think it's very clear what the chain is going for in terms of technical goals and we have this IBRL that's guiding technical direction. How do you guys think about things from a business or strategic perspective and what the non-technical constraints for Solana are?

Anatoly Yakovenko: I always kind of thought crypto as being this next iteration of the internet. The biggest constraint is just uh human understanding. So like trying to explain to my parents what private key cryptography is and like seed phrases and all all of these things, it just takes time. Um, and I think every way that you can get somebody to engage with these technologies, um, is a net good. So meme coins, NFTs, whatever gets that person's interest sparked and they go install their favorite wallet, Solflare or whatever, and go interact with the chain, I think that that's a net good.

Mary Gooneratne: What's your de facto explanation for blockchain when your mom's friend asks what you do?

Anatoly Yakovenko: Um... I I that one's tough. Like I I think the easiest... the easiest way to explain it is like childhood trauma and leaving the Soviet Union. And that you can take your your assets with you when you gotta flee. But yeah.

Mary Gooneratne: I'm sure that clarifies a lot. And kind of thinking about the converse side of that, what do you think are the two to three big technical improvements that'll be kind of the major unlocks?

Anatoly Yakovenko: I mean, um, Firedancer full client is here, that that's awesome. So having it kind of the final audits and that like final step is really great to see. That, in my mind, this is where I've always felt like is the time to rip off the beta tag is when we have two implementations of the protocols. So there's no bus factor and we have this kind of true uh interpretation of of what it means to do like a valid state transition on the network. So this to me is like, you know, I don't think we need the beta tag anymore. And obviously the IBRL improvements that everyone has been working on, AlpenGlow and MCP, those hopefully going live uh um relatively soon next year.

Mary Gooneratne: Another question I had, maybe Raj this one's for you, but I think over again this past year another conversation that's come up over over and over again has been the role of revenue on chain and across the ecosystem. And I'm curious both what do you where do you think this next like 2x in revenue comes from and whether that's a metric that you guys kind of are prioritizing or what metrics you guys are prioritizing?

Raj Gokal: Yeah, I mean I I think Toly really drilled it into all of our heads that, you know, a a revenue generating business is kind of the the closest thing you can get to a source of truth as as you can in um, you know, in in focusing a team. So the more revenue generating businesses we have uh on chain that, you know, grow and and thrive independently with use cases to make use of block space, the better. Um, I think, you know, a recent chart that came out that we look at as a a huge breakthrough is is, you know, if you look at the last um 20 companies that uh that reached 100 million dollars in revenue, um the majority of them are crypto businesses. I don't think a lot of people knew that. Uh and the majority of those are on Solana. So that was always kind of the the goal. Where it comes from, I think um you need the entropy of like thousands of entrepreneurs um just exploring every possible um value proposition for how to use block space. And I think we have that. Like Colosseum hackathons are just getting bigger and bigger, 1700 teams at a time now. Um, and uh, you know, I think we just want to see more of that.

Mary Gooneratne: And what other metrics in addition to revenue do you are is kind of forefront or you think are kind of upstream of revenue?

Raj Gokal: What do you think, Toly?

Anatoly Yakovenko: I mean, um, every every business is going to have kind of its secondary metrics, but I think for the L1 and the financial use case specifically, volumes and TVL and all those kind of DeFi specific metrics are really important. Um, but you still I think need to tie it back to like how does this business make money? How does it because that really translates to how are you creating value for the end user, for your customer, and how much runway you have. Like ultimately I think um the market cap of all of crypto is going to continue to grow, but at some point I think the value is going to be split by revenue.

Mary Gooneratne: And do does the context of that change at all when you think about the Seeker and that product and revenue on the Seeker for developers on Seeker?

Anatoly Yakovenko: Yeah, this has been I think a really pleasant surprise how fast we've seen teams actually start to earn revenues uh on Seeker and the SMS platform. Um, so we're seeing that like CFL, Perena, uh Moonwalk Fitness, like Ore Miner, these apps launched and they got their first 10,000 users uh in the Seeker ecosystem. And this is again a much much smaller distribution channel than Apple, but it's a very concentrated one. These are folks that are crypto first, crypto native, and they want to try stuff. So if you're building a mobile app and you need your first 10,000 users, this is a very small pond which you can be a big fish and um get that iteration and and figure out those loops that get your users excited and start earning your first dollar. I think that that's really important unlock for developers.

Mary Gooneratne: Yeah, it's a I feel like a pretty hard to reach audience in general just with kind of the anonymity in the space and you do have that reach on social media, but reaching those like prosumers of crypto is harder. And we've been getting more requests for Seeker, um for Loopscale, and so it's we gotta prioritize that mobile application. Um, and so want to also just kind of think about maybe it could be a Seeker application, but if you guys were not the founders of Solana and Solana existed today, what would you guys want to build on Solana?

Raj Gokal: I, you know, every time I have a an answer to that question, if I scroll enough on Twitter, I see that somebody is already building it on on Solana, which is a great great sign. I think the latest one is um uh more gamified um trading interfaces for for options. Because it seems like I think anything that makes finance more fun is um I think aligned with where crypto is heading. Um, you know, I think in 10 years people will have a less toxic and, you know, scary relationship with finance because of crypto. So, you know, right now where I think the the frontier for that is is in in more fun trading interfaces. And I think there's um a Colosseum team called Banana Zone that made one. It's like, it's awesome. Everybody should go use it.

Mary Gooneratne: Are you excited for the 16 replies shilling their apps after this?

Raj Gokal: More. Everybody should be shilling.

Mary Gooneratne: And what about yourself?

Anatoly Yakovenko: Um, I would take like the for the proof-of-work based Ore Miner and do like a superconductor search for the proof-of-work part. Do you want to elaborate on that a bit? I think like uh the other side of the finance stuff, just go have fun. Like come up with a crazy idea and the there's a lot of weird crypto loops that don't work in any other place in the world. Uh I think having like using the proof-of-work mechanisms for getting people to go participate in the network to do something I think is really interesting and under explored. And when that stuff pops off, you get like you create whole new categories like um um what what was it uh walk-to-earn or whatever with uh... STEPN. That those things happen and all of a sudden you get a thousand new devs trying to replicate that in other parts in in other parts of application development and that's how innovation goes. So.

Mary Gooneratne: Do you think kind of in that same vein there's advice for founders specific to cryp that you think applies specifically or especially in crypto?

Anatoly Yakovenko: Um, I think uh you gotta ship fast. Like that would be my my advice is um iterate and try out an idea, get it to market, and like watch it fail and try to find those couple users that do um something interesting with with what you built and then kind of reshape the product around it.

Mary Gooneratne: And want to also ask, Raj, you had tweeted a a few weeks ago "what are Solana's 10-year goals?" but it was just a quote tweet of Elon and you didn't answer the question. So I would love to hear kind of what you guys view as the vision for Solana say 10 years from now that makes you guys most excited about having built the chain.

Raj Gokal: Yeah, I mean I I think the when we when we used to talk about the scale of the vision, um the metric we always talked about was what percent of global finance could be on chain. Um, I you know I think Solana is positioned to accelerate its share of how much crypto gets uh over the next 10 years. And so I think it's reasonable and also I think crypto is accelerating faster than any of us anticipated. In 2017 we didn't imagine we'd have such a clear US regulatory environment, that, you know, we'd get the support of major countries um, you know, issuing assets on chain, major banks. Um, so I think the I think we should probably upgrade that to like 25% and, you know, Solana gets at least 10% um in in that trade. So.

Mary Gooneratne: Or all 25%?

Raj Gokal: Yeah, maybe all 25. But I think there's room for lots of, you know, lots of competition. Competition is good.

Mary Gooneratne: Do you feel like your dream 10-year vision is relatively aligned?

Anatoly Yakovenko: Um, I can't think of more than like eight weeks ahead.

Mary Gooneratne: Very fair. And I I rumor has it that you guys have some announcements to make this morning.

Raj Gokal: Yeah, just just one important one. Um, I think first of all we wanted to thank everybody for coming out to Abu Dhabi, especially people from as far as California that flew 16 hours. I think everyone's um big question is what, you know, what's next. So we have a video to um tell you where we're gonna gonna all meet up next year.

[Video plays showing London, November 15-17, 2026, at Olympia]

Raj Gokal: Seems like everybody's pretty happy with with that choice. Um, yeah. I mean I just to we we it's it's hard to pick a a location. I think the the events team, um shout out to to Ellie and and Akshay who just do an incredible job putting these on. It's it's a very difficult choice. You have to optimize for visas, you have to look forward ahead one year and um and think about, you know, strategic positioning for the for the ecosystem, for the network, um and for everybody who builds on it. Um, we're we're pretty confident London's gonna be an an excellent Breakpoint for for next year. Um, just as Abu Dhabi was this year. So with that, um thanks so much for coming guys.

Anatoly Yakovenko: Yeah, thank you everyone.

tags.impact_reason: A panel discussion with Solana founders reflecting on the network's evolution and future, highlighting community growth and diverse use cases.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose the 'Firedancer' as the Semantic Anchor because it is the literal name of the software client upgrade central to the story, and it serves as a powerful metaphor for Solana's next phase: energetic, performant, and gracefully handling complex tasks. This personifies the network's evolution. I included the text 'BETA' being shattered because this is the core narrative—the end of the beta era. Making it diegetic, etched on a physical pane being destroyed by the Firedancer, directly visualizes this pivotal transition from a trial phase to a mature, robust mainnet.
visual_idea.description: A wide shot of an abstract humanoid figure—the Firedancer—composed of swirling, energetic trails of orange, purple, and green light. It is captured mid-motion in a dynamic, powerful pose, as if kicking through a large pane of crystalline glass. Etched into the glass is the word 'BETA', which is now fracturing and exploding outwards from the point of impact. The background is a vast, dark, cavernous space made of polished obsidian, with faint, distant geometric server architecture receding into the deep background, emphasizing immense scale. The scene is lit by the Firedancer itself, casting dramatic, volumetric light and reflections onto the glossy floor.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, wide shot, portrait-oriented (3:4 aspect ratio) composition of an abstract humanoid figure—the Firedancer—composed entirely of swirling, energetic Holographic Light Trails in vibrant orange, purple, and green. The Firedancer is captured mid-motion in a dynamic, powerful pose, as if kicking through a large pane of crystalline glass. Etched deeply into the crystalline glass is the word 'BETA', which is spectacularly fracturing and exploding outwards from the point of impact, shards of light scattering. The background reveals a vast, dark, cavernous space crafted from seamlessly Polished Obsidian, creating highly reflective surfaces that mirror the Firedancer's glow and casting deep, dramatic shadows. Faint, distant geometric server architecture, embodying a Techno-Organic aesthetic, recedes into the deep background, emphasizing immense scale. The scene is lit by the Firedancer itself, which emits intense Volumetric Neon Lighting, casting dramatic, ethereal light beams and vivid reflections onto the glossy obsidian floor, emphasizing height and depth with significant negative space above the figure to enhance its monumental presence. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic lighting.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose the 'Firedancer' as the Semantic Anchor because it is the literal name of the software client upgrade central to the story, and it serves as a powerful metaphor for Solana's next phase: energetic, performant, and gracefully handling complex tasks. This personifies the network's evolution. I included the text 'BETA' being shattered because this is the core narrative—the end of the beta era. Making it diegetic, etched on a physical pane being destroyed by the Firedancer, directly visualizes this pivotal transition from a trial phase to a mature, robust mainnet.
visual_idea.description: A macro close-up on the 'foot' of the Firedancer. This is not a literal foot, but a swirling vortex of concentrated holographic data and fiery light particles at the moment of impact. We see the intricate weaving of countless light trails, pulsing with immense energy against a sliver of the shattering crystalline pane. The background is a dark, out-of-focus plane of polished obsidian, allowing the texture and raw power of the light trails to be the absolute focus.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro close-up on the 'foot' of the Firedancer. This is not a literal foot, but a swirling vortex of concentrated holographic data and fiery light particles at the moment of impact. We see the intricate weaving of countless holographic light trails, pulsing with immense volumetric neon lighting and techno-organic energy against a sliver of the shattering crystalline pane. The background is a dark, out-of-focus plane of polished obsidian, allowing the texture and raw power of the light trails to be the absolute focus. Compose for a square aspect ratio (1:1), emphasizing symmetry and central focus with tight macro framing, filling the frame with intricate texture. Ultra-realistic dramatic lighting with high-contrast, featuring soft neon mixed lighting and strong neon reflections. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, volumetric lighting.

editorial.headline: Solana Founders Signal End of 'Beta' Era With Firedancer Launch
editorial.standfirst: Anatoly Yakovenko and Raj Gokal outline the roadmap to capturing 25% of global finance, prioritizing client diversity and revenue-generating protocols. The network's 'beta tag' will be removed following the full deployment of Firedancer.
key_takeaways: Anatoly Yakovenko confirmed the 'beta tag' will be removed once the **Firedancer** full client is live, eliminating the network's 'bus factor' risk.
Raj Gokal revealed that the majority of the last 20 crypto companies to reach $100M in revenue are built on **Solana**.
**Breakpoint 2026** was officially announced for London, scheduled for November 15-17, 2026.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
For institutional investors awaiting a signal that **Solana** is ready for critical infrastructure, Anatoly Yakovenko just delivered it. The co-founder explicitly stated that the network’s long-standing "beta" designation will be removed following the full launch of **Firedancer**, a second independent validator client. This move addresses the critical "bus factor" risk—a primary concern for asset managers—by ensuring liveness through client diversity. With the **IBRL** (Increase Bandwidth, Reduce Latency) technical roadmap guiding development, upcoming upgrades like **AlpenGlow** and **MCP** (Multiple Concurrent Proposers) are set to cement the network's capacity for global-scale finance.

The conversation also shifted the primary valuation metric from ephemeral TVL to hard revenue. Raj Gokal highlighted a striking statistic for VCs: of the last 20 crypto companies to hit $100 million in revenue, the majority are built on Solana. This revenue-first reality is being driven by a new class of applications leveraging the **Seeker** mobile device as a high-conversion user acquisition channel. Early movers like **Perena**, **CFL** (Crypto Fantasy League), and **Moonwalk Fitness** are utilizing the mobile ecosystem to secure their first 10,000 "prosumer" users, proving that mobile-native distribution is a viable alternative to traditional app stores.

Looking beyond the immediate horizon, the founders set a target for 25% of global finance to eventually move on-chain, with Solana positioned to capture a significant plurality of that volume. Innovation remains aggressive; Yakovenko pointed to **Ore**, a proof-of-work project on Solana, as a template for new incentive mechanisms, while Gokal praised **Banana Zone**, a **Colosseum** hackathon winner, for pioneering gamified trading interfaces. As the ecosystem matures from "internet for cats" to a financial rail capable of settlement at the speed of physics, the message to capital allocators is clear: the infrastructure phase is concluding, and the deployment phase has begun.
